serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Gula Melaka Tang Yuan consists of approximately 6 glutinous rice balls served in warm gula melaka (coconut palm sugar) syrup with coconut milk, weighing approximately 200 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Glutinous Rice Ball (Tang Yuan) with Palm Sugar Syrup — estimated from component analysis using IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia MyFCD; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Gula Melaka Tang Yuan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis: glutinous rice flour (IMR MyFCD / USDA SR Legacy USDA #20067), gula melaka / palm sugar (IMR MyFCD; USDA SR Legacy #19335 brown sugar as proxy), and coconut milk (USDA SR Legacy #12118). Proportions estimated as approximately 45% glutinous rice dough, 35% gula melaka syrup, 20% coconut milk by weight. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a standard preparation.

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice products GI — analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (GI ~86) and palm sugar syrup dilution effect; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gula Melaka Tang Yuan. Glutinous rice has a published GI of approximately 86 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008). The addition of gula melaka (palm sugar, GI ~35 per University of Sydney GI Database) and coconut milk (negligible GI contribution) in the syrup dilutes the overall glycemic response. A weighted composite GI of approximately 61 is estimated based on the carbohydrate contribution of each component: glutinous rice dough (~45% weight, ~80% of carbohydrate at GI 86) and gula melaka syrup (~35% weight, ~20% of carbohydrate at GI 35). GL per 200 g serving = (61 × 77.0 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 47.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~18 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Gula Melaka Tang Yuan is low in sodium (~36 mg per 200 g serving) and does not pose a significant hypertensive risk; however, portion control is advised due to its high sugar and energy density.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~61, medium GI) and high glycemic load (~47 per serving) driven by glutinous rice flour and palm sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Gula Melaka Tang Yuan sparingly, as the high glycemic load (~47 per standard serving) from glutinous rice flour and palm sugar can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from coconut milk (~3.9 g per 100 g) and high sugar content (~18 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from coconut milk and the high free sugar content in Gula Melaka Tang Yuan are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and be mindful of cumulative saturated fat and added sugar intake across the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gula Melaka Tang Yuan is a Malaysian-Singaporean dessert consisting of smooth, chewy glutinous rice flour balls served in a warm, fragrant syrup made from gula melaka (coconut palm sugar) and pandan leaves, often enriched with coconut milk. The glutinous rice dough is hand-rolled into small spheres, boiled until they float, and then served in the dark, caramel-toned palm sugar syrup. The texture of the balls is soft, sticky, and pleasantly chewy, while the syrup is deeply sweet with earthy, caramel-like notes characteristic of gula melaka, complemented by the subtle floral aroma of pandan and the richness of coconut milk. Tang Yuan is traditionally associated with the Chinese Dongzhi (Winter Solstice) festival and is widely consumed across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia, including Singapore and Malaysia. The Gula Melaka variant is a distinctly Malaysian-Singaporean adaptation that replaces the conventional plain or red bean filling with the local palm sugar syrup preparation. Nutritionally, this dessert is energy-dense (~198 kcal per 100 g), high in carbohydrates (~38.5 g per 100 g) predominantly from glutinous rice starch and palm sugar, and contains moderate saturated fat (~3.9 g per 100 g) from coconut milk. The glycemic load per standard serving is high (~47), making it a significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Sodium content is low (~18 mg per 100 g), posing minimal hypertensive risk. The dish is naturally vegan, halal, and dairy-free, though it contains coconut as a tree nut allergen.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Gula Melaka Tang Yuan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis using IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy for individual ingredients (glutinous rice flour, gula melaka/palm sugar, coconut milk). GI is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for glutinous rice and palm sugar from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is medium due to the estimation methodology rather than a direct food composition measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy; no direct food composition entry for Gula Melaka Tang Yuan exists in any approved primary source. Values should be treated as central estimates with ±15% uncertainty. 2. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this dish has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the access date. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this preparation. 4. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy entries used for component estimation. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this preparation. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this preparation. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this preparation. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): glutinous rice flour, palm sugar, and coconut milk do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data. 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero): all ingredients are plant-derived; no animal products are used in the standard preparation. 10. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): this is a plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. 11. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): plant-based ingredients contain no vitamin D; naturally zero for this preparation. 12. The cuisine_tags in the input (Curry Rice, Nasi Lemak) appear to be platform-level restaurant tags unrelated to this specific dish and have not been carried forward. 13. Serving size of 200 g (approximately 6 pieces with syrup) is estimated based on typical restaurant portion sizes for tang yuan in Singapore and Malaysia.
